EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, dynamic system dug into the southern and central plains, and a swath of warm and extreme dry air punched into portions of Oklahoma along with south-central and southeast Kansas. This very dry air was accompanied by very strong southwesterly winds with gusts between 58 and 77 mph. These strong winds caused damage across a large portion of southern and eastern Kansas. Additionally, several large wildfires occurred as a result of dry and very windy conditions, one of which burned down a retirement home in Yates Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest wind gust was 63 mph from a Union Pacific weather station at 2:58 PM CDT located about three miles west of Canton. An additional wind gust of 61 mph was also recorded in McPherson a bit earlier at 1:55 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another very strong system impacted a large portions of the High Plains and Central Plains from the early morning hours to the early afternoon hours of March 19. Strong southerly winds occurred ahead of a strong approaching cold front during the early morning hours across portions of south-central and southeast Kansas. Then a strong cold front and strong northerly winds that accompanied it moved into central Kansas later that morning. These strong winds continued to spread southward into south-central Kansas. Meanwhile, moderate to heavy snow developed across portions of western and central Kansas and produced blizzard conditions during much of the morning and early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS site in Great Bend began reporting 40 mph sustained winds around 7:15 AM CDT. A peak gust of 62 mph was measured at 9:56 AM CDT. Sustained winds of 40 mph generally came to an end around 2:00 PM CDT. Additionally, power outages were reported across the county, and a tree was uprooted in Great Bend as reported by the county emergency manager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe storms impacted much of the eastern half of Kansas during the afternoon hours of May 19th into the early morning hours of the 20th. During the early afternoon hours of May 19th, a couple of rounds of severe storms impacted southeast Kansas. Both rounds of severe storms moved north out of northeast Oklahoma and produced extensive tree damage with winds up to 70 mph as well as hail up to half-dollar size. Additional storms developed across portions of south central Kansas and the Flint Hills during the early evening hours of May 19th. These storms produced up to tennis ball sized hail along with 60 mph winds. Later during the evening and nighttime hours of May 19th, a couple of clusters of storms moved out of western Kansas and into central and south central Kansas. This round of severe storms produced up to 91 mph winds and half-dollar sized hail. ||Additionally, non-thunderstorm high winds occurred across much of south central Kansas behind the final round of severe storms, and several reports of damage along with non-thunderstorm wind gusts up to 72 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe weather impacted many areas of Kansas. The initial round developed during the mid-afternoon hours with numerous large hail reports across central and south-central Kansas. The next round developed during the late afternoon and evening hours, producing large hail across central Kansas. ||The final round developed after 9 PM across west-central Kansas and moved east-northeastward into Reno County. A lone supercell thunderstorm tracked across southwest Kansas and eventually moved into Reno County. This storm produced a long track wedge tornado that was on the ground for over 30 miles and had a width around a mile wide. It destroyed three homes near the town of Plevna along with widespread tree and power line damage. This tornado was given an EF-3 rating with the most significant damage just southwest of Plevna. Fortunately there were no injuries or fatalities with this destructive tornado.

Several rounds of severe storms impacted much of the eastern half of Kansas during the afternoon hours of May 19th into the early morning hours of the 20th. During the early afternoon hours of May 19th, a couple of rounds of severe storms impacted southeast Kansas. Both rounds of severe storms moved north out of northeast Oklahoma and produced extensive tree damage with winds up to 70 mph as well as hail up to half-dollar size. Additional storms developed across portions of south central Kansas and the Flint Hills during the early evening hours of May 19th. These storms produced up to tennis ball sized hail along with 60 mph winds. Later during the evening and nighttime hours of May 19th, a couple of clusters of storms moved out of western Kansas and into central and south central Kansas. This round of severe storms produced up to 91 mph winds and half-dollar sized hail. ||Additionally, non-thunderstorm high winds occurred across much of south central Kansas behind the final round of severe storms, and several reports of damage along with non-thunderstorm wind gusts up to 72 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a measured wind gust from a trained spotter with an anemometer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on the NWS storm survey, the tornado started in Pratt County then went into Stafford County before entering western Reno County. The total track of the tornado was around 32 miles with 21 of those in Reno County. A peak of EF3 damage was found with estimated winds of 155 mph occurring in Stafford and Pratt counties. In Reno County, the peak estimated winds were 145 mph based on a brick exterior home that had all of the exterior walls removed except long entryway. One portion of an interior wall remained. The maximum width was around one mile. This tornado damaged multiple homes to varying degrees in the town of Plevna with damage ranging from EF0 to EF2. Then the torando tracked northeast before going northwest prior to dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of western Kansas during the evening hours of May 23rd. These thunderstorms merged into a line and moved southeast across portions central, south central, and southeast Kansas during the morning hours of May 24th. Additional thunderstorms developed in advance of the line of thunderstorms from northeast Reno County into Sedgwick and Butler counties. Fortunately, severe weather and damage was very limited with only two reports of severe criteria wind gusts being reported at the Wellington Airport and in Caney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system pushed into the area during the afternoon of May 19, 2025 before pushing into Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours. This system produced quarter size hail, wind damage, and up to 3 inches of rainfall in southeast Kansas before continuing on to produce 8 tornadoes and widespread damaging winds in Missouri. The intense rainfall led to some localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees were down over Northwest Belleview Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight, in the early morning hours of June 17th, Large Thunderstorms over the I-70 corridor of wet central Kansas congealed into a larger more organized complex, before propagating southeast into central and southcentral Kansas during the overnight. This convective complex produced wind gusts as high as 72 mph in Ellis county, as well as wind damage reports through around 3 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The measured gust was reported by the Kansas State Mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving, forward propagating MCS developed late in the evening of June 13th over northeast Colorado, before spreading into western Kansas in the early morning hours of June 14th.  Numerous severe thunderstorms wind gusts were reported between about 3 and 7 am, as well as copious amount of rainfall in the two to three inches range across south central Kansas in the pre-dawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of one inch hail was received from a social media post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight, in the early morning hours of June 17th, Large Thunderstorms over the I-70 corridor of wet central Kansas congealed into a larger more organized complex, before propagating southeast into central and southcentral Kansas during the overnight. This convective complex produced wind gusts as high as 72 mph in Ellis county, as well as wind damage reports through around 3 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report was received from the Kansas State Mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening a group of thunderstorms moved northeast through part of Northwest Kansas.  The main threat with these storms was straight-line winds with the storm activity, which continued for some locations after the thunderstorms had ended.  The straight-line winds from these thunderstorms continued to produce an expanding outflow boundary as the storm activity moved in Kansas.  Wind gusts of 60-86 MPH were reported as the outflow boundary moved through, with gusts around 70 MPH reported even after the thunderstorm activity had moved on.  The highest wind gust of 86 MPH was reported north of Ludell.  With the dry soils, dust storm conditions were reported as the outflow boundary moved through.  The dust storm lasted for roughly 30 minutes before ending.  A few of the thunderstorms managed to produce large hail, with the largest being half dollar size at St. Francis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous two and a half inch diameter tree limbs blown down across the property.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe wind gusts extending out ahead of thunderstorms caused a dust storm to move east across the county behind an outflow boundary from the thunderstorm activity. A deputy reported zero visibility due to blowing dust at the intersection of CR 15 and 77.  Quarter mile visibility reported in the dust. This is west of the CR 75.2 and CR 28 intersection.  The dust storm conditions lasted around 10 minutes or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a trough from a deep low entered the area, a line of clustered storms developed and moved into the area. One long lasting cluster moved through the northern counties producing both severe winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 72 mph at the mesonet station KKSDIGHT22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an approaching low and dynamically primed atmosphere, storms were able to develop and take advantage of ample moisture. The event began with fairly isolated supercells moving primarily eastward before a line of storms took over descending from the northwest. The storms produced numerous hail reports and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kearny county emergency manager reported 1 inch hail.

A regional outbreak of tornadoes occurred across portions|of western into south-central Kansas during the afternoon to|evening hours of Sunday 18 May 2025.  The first couple of tornadic|supercells occurred near Scott City KS in Scott County during the|late afternoon and progressed northward with both storms generating|EF2 tornadoes and a lone brief EFU tornado. A second tornadic|supercell developed near Dodge City KS in the early evening and|progressed northeast with an EFO tornado occurring near Kinsley KS|in Edwards County and an EF2 tornado near Garfield KS in Pawnee County.|A final long-duration tornadic supercell then formed in the mid|evening hours over Comanche County KS and commenced moving northeast|toward Greensburg KS in Kiowa County, Iuka KS in Pratt County,|north of Preston KS in Pratt County and finally across Stafford|and Reno Counties. This storm generated five EF3 tornadoes along with|several smaller satellite tornadoes.  The final count for this day|was 13 tornadoes just within the NWS Dodge City Warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser captured a small satellite tornado off of the large Pratt tornado with pictures and video. No damage was reported or found.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public and storm chasers reported a tornado. A survey determined that a swath of damage was due to the tornado. Some damage included striped trees, broken power poles, a destroyed metal building, and large roof damage to a residential building. The tornado began in Pratt county before moving into Stafford county and then continued into ICT's CWA.

The dust storm lasted for roughly 30 minutes before ending.  A few of the thunderstorms managed to produce large hail, with the largest being half dollar size at St. Francis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of less than 100 yard visibility in a wall of dust along thunderstorm outflow boundary.  The dust storm conditions began east of Highway 25, continuing across the east half of the county.  Meanwhile thunderstorm activity continued to move northeast over the northwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms erupted along a stationary front during the early evening hours of April 22nd from south central Kansas into western Oklahoma. With plenty of instability and shear, storms quickly became severe as they tracked off to the northeast. We received numerous reports of golf ball sized hail with hail up to tennis ball size reported in Reno County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWCH provided report of ping pong ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon to evening hours, scattered storms developed and trained across portions of northeast Kansas, leading to isolated reports of Flash Flooding with up to 5 inches of rainfall reported. These storms also produced hail to baseball size, wind gusts to 70 mph, and a brief tornado in Pottawatomie county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed by Kansas State EM. A foot of water on the roadways at Kansas State and in Manhattan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: What appeared to begin as a gustnado became connected to the storm updraft at least briefly along the leading edge of a thunderstorm as it moved through the small town of Belvue. The small vortex moved ENE for a few miles doing minor damage to trees and outbuildings before dissipating as strong outflow winds overwhelmed it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed in central Kansas and moved into portions of north central Kansas during the late evening hours. While most storms overnight were not severe, a few produced hail to ping pong ball size in Ottawa county.

In the late afternoon of the 21st, a cold front moved into northwestern Kansas from the northwest. A brief 58 MPH gust was observed at the Atwood, KS AWOS.  Meanwhile scattered showers developed.  Most of the rain from these showers evaporated before reaching the ground.  This caused brief, sporadic wind gusts to occur under these showers.  The dry ground allowed blowing dust to occur with many of these sporadic wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atwood AWOS recorded a gust of 58 MPH as the front went through. Patchy blowing dust was reported, but no significant reports of blowing dust were passed along.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm developed then intensified over eastern Saline County during the late afternoon into the early evening of the 28th. The storm tracked northeast and produced two inch hail near New Cambria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWCH relayed a picture sent in by a viewer of two inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms erupted along a stationary front during the early evening hours of April 22nd from south central Kansas into western Oklahoma. Strong south winds quickly increased low level moisture on Tuesday April 1st while a cold front sagged down from the north. This set the stage for severe storms to erupt during the early evening hour across central Kansas. One storm along the line quickly became a supercell and started to exhibit low level rotation. It produced a couple brief tornadoes as it approached Salina but luckily lifted before reaching the city. Numerous additional storms produced large hail along with damaging winds.

These storms also produced hail to baseball size, wind gusts to 70 mph, and a brief tornado in Pottawatomie county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outbuilding was damaged.  Winds were estimated.

In the afternoon of the 23rd, a line of storms formed across western Kansas and slowly moved north-northeast. The more intense storms produced hail up to golf ball size. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper-level shortwave ejected into the southern Great Plains. Southeasterly flow amplified the moisture available to the developing storms in addition to CAPE values in excess of 2000 J/kg. 0-6 km shear values were around 30 kts with hodograph soundings indicating good lower level shear with slight backing in the mid levels. The only severe output recorded from these storms were severe hail across the afternoon and evening of 4/24 with heavy rain lasting into the morning of 4/25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of 1 inch hail.

In the late afternoon of the 2nd, a cluster of storms formed in far western Kansas and proceeded to the north-northeast. Across western Kansas, these storms produced winds up to 80 MPH and penny sized hail. The strongest winds were associated with an outflow boundary coming from the cluster that also produced a haboob (wall of dust) that reduced visibility down to zero. This was a unique system because the overall system and wall of dust generally moved from south to north.  A short landspout tornado occurred ahead of the wall of dust in Sherman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWN site Section 22 reported a 64 mph wind gust.

Although most of this activity remained sub-severe, a thunderstorm over Cherokee County was able to reach severe limits, bringing a strong wind gust of 62 mph northeast of Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a 62mph measured wind gust in Cherokee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous storms develop across northwest Kansas during the evening of the 28th. Recorded wind gusts around Leoti reported 68-71 mph winds, with a trained spotter estimating 60 mph winds. Damage was also reported in the town of Leoti from the Emergency Manager.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reports a microburst with broken poles and downed tree limbs around 6 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 16th, two lines of storms formed, one in northwestern Kansas, the other in eastern Colorado. The northwestern Kansas storms slowly moved to the north-northeast as the Colorado line moved to the northeast. As these two lines collided, additional storms formed near the I-70 corridor. Storms lasted for over 5 hours, produced winds up to 80 to 90 MPH, causing damage at a brick car wash in St. Francis and causing visibility to drop to near zero from blowing dust. Additionally, hail up to 1 inch was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated and coming out of the southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was situated over central Kansas, with a quasi-stationary frontal boundary draped across northwest Missouri down through the Texas and Oklahoma panhandles. Destabilization occurred during the early afternoon on August 11, 2025, with showers and thunderstorms developing along an outflow boundary pushing east northeast through southeast Kansas into southwest Missouri. Although most of this activity remained sub-severe, an isolated severe thunderstorm brought damaging winds to Cherokee County, with reports of trees and tree limbs down southeast of Lawton near the Cherokee/Jasper county line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of trees and tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 16th, two lines of storms formed, one in northwestern Kansas, the other in eastern Colorado. The northwestern Kansas storms slowly moved to the north-northeast as the Colorado line moved to the northeast. As these two lines collided, additional storms formed near the I-70 corridor. Storms lasted for over 5 hours, produced winds up to 80 to 90 MPH, causing damage at a brick car wash in St. Francis and causing visibility to drop to near zero from blowing dust. Additionally, hail up to 1 inch was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported via Weather Underground station kkskanor6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 2nd, a cluster of storms formed in far western Kansas and proceeded to the north-northeast. Across western Kansas, these storms produced winds up to 80 MPH and penny sized hail. The strongest winds were associated with an outflow boundary coming from the cluster that also produced a haboob (wall of dust) that reduced visibility down to zero. This was a unique system because the overall system and wall of dust generally moved from south to north.  A short landspout tornado occurred ahead of the wall of dust in Sherman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inch diameter tree limb was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moved over western Kansas early in the morning. Correlating with a PVU maximum and left exit region of the jet aloft, plenty of dynamical support was present for strong storms to develop producing all hazards. This included a weak and brief tornado, 4 inch hail, and 85 mph winds. This manifested in a couple supercells and a small bowing MCS moving southward across SW Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report was from the Kansas State Mesonet of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low pressure system was in NW Kansas with a frontal boundary draped across western Kansas and into Kansas. The mesoscale ingredients were not too favorable for widespread convection development although lapse rates were potent enough for downburst/heat burst generation. A heat burst from convection in the Oklahoma reached into one of the counties in Kansas with severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat burst winds at Mesonet station UR350 Kismws recorded a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an upper-level trough over the Rockies lending support, thunderstorms developed in eastern Colorado. The storms reached the Kansas border in the early morning of 8/10. A marginally severe gust was recorded in Hamilton county before the bulk of the system developed a cold pool generating more severe winds in Lane and Ellis counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station K3K3 Syracuse recorded a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough was situated such that strong ascent will be supported. While the strongest surface forcing is farther west, enough convection initiated and moving eastward into the CWA. While staying discrete at first, it quickly developed into a MCS. Through the middle of the night a cohesive cold pool developed and generated 2 80+ mph wind gust reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site recorded a 81 mph wind gust.

Correlating with a PVU maximum and left exit region of the jet aloft, plenty of dynamical support was present for strong storms to develop producing all hazards. This included a weak and brief tornado, 4 inch hail, and 85 mph winds. This manifested in a couple supercells and a small bowing MCS moving southward across SW Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station UP367 4.9 NE Meade (UPR) reported a 66mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough was positioned in northern Kansas with a 700 mb temperature gradient stretching into SW Kansas with a wedge of high moisture. On the afternoon of the 27th, supercells initiated and produced severe hail and winds. Into the evening, it became a more multicellular regime with widespread heavy rainfall. This produced flash flooding in multiple counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported 1 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low pressure system was in NW Kansas with a frontal boundary draped across western Kansas and into Kansas. The mesoscale ingredients were not too favorable for widespread convection development although lapse rates were potent enough for downburst/heat burst generation. A heat burst from convection in the Oklahoma reached into one of the counties in Kansas with severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat burst winds Mesonet station UP352 2.7 SW Liberal (UPR) recorded a 57 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough was situated such that strong ascent will be supported. While the strongest surface forcing is farther west, enough convection initiated and moving eastward into the CWA. While staying discrete at first, it quickly developed into a MCS. Through the middle of the night a cohesive cold pool developed and generated 2 80+ mph wind gust reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station, Kents station, recorded a 71 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely intense low pressure system moved into Missouri during the evening of March 4th.  Northwest winds on the backside of the storm increased and rain changed to snow bringing a brief 3-4 hour period of blizzard conditions to parts of northeast Kansas. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1-3 inches however northwest winds of 40-70 mph combined with falling snow to reduce visibilities to one quarter mile or less in open areas for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video taken by a storm chaser showed near zero visibilities in heavy falling and blowing snow and winds of 40-70 mph in Fairview Kansas on highway 36.  This corresponded to area ASOS that showed visibilities also falling to between one half and one quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 2nd, a cluster of storms formed in far western Kansas and proceeded to the north-northeast. Across western Kansas, these storms produced winds up to 80 MPH and penny sized hail. The strongest winds were associated with an outflow boundary coming from the cluster that also produced a haboob (wall of dust) that reduced visibility down to zero. This was a unique system because the overall system and wall of dust generally moved from south to north.  A short landspout tornado occurred ahead of the wall of dust in Sherman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWN Mesonet reported a 62 MPH wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A regional outbreak of tornadoes occurred across portions|of western into south-central Kansas during the afternoon to|evening hours of Sunday 18 May 2025.  The first couple of tornadic|supercells occurred near Scott City KS in Scott County during the|late afternoon and progressed northward with both storms generating|EF2 tornadoes and a lone brief EFU tornado. A second tornadic|supercell developed near Dodge City KS in the early evening and|progressed northeast with an EFO tornado occurring near Kinsley KS|in Edwards County and an EF2 tornado near Garfield KS in Pawnee County.|A final long-duration tornadic supercell then formed in the mid|evening hours over Comanche County KS and commenced moving northeast|toward Greensburg KS in Kiowa County, Iuka KS in Pratt County,|north of Preston KS in Pratt County and finally across Stafford|and Reno Counties. This storm generated five EF3 tornadoes along with|several smaller satellite tornadoes.  The final count for this day|was 13 tornadoes just within the NWS Dodge City Warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 1.75 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A regional outbreak of tornadoes occurred across portions|of western into south-central Kansas during the afternoon to|evening hours of Sunday 18 May 2025.  The first couple of tornadic|supercells occurred near Scott City KS in Scott County during the|late afternoon and progressed northward with both storms generating|EF2 tornadoes and a lone brief EFU tornado. A second tornadic|supercell developed near Dodge City KS in the early evening and|progressed northeast with an EFO tornado occurring near Kinsley KS|in Edwards County and an EF2 tornado near Garfield KS in Pawnee County.|A final long-duration tornadic supercell then formed in the mid|evening hours over Comanche County KS and commenced moving northeast|toward Greensburg KS in Kiowa County, Iuka KS in Pratt County,|north of Preston KS in Pratt County and finally across Stafford|and Reno Counties. This storm generated five EF3 tornadoes along with|several smaller satellite tornadoes.  The final count for this day|was 13 tornadoes just within the NWS Dodge City Warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado began just south of W Road 270 in northern Scott County. The tornado tracked northward toward the Scott Wildlife Area where damage was observed to trees, including snapped trunks and a total destruction of an outbuilding. The tornado continued north into Logan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front filtered Arctic air across the High Plains with numerous days of -20 to -30 wind chills. A power outage across portions of Tribune in Greeley county was also thought to have been related to the rapid falling temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported that part of NW Greeley county|experienced a brief power outage due to the wind. The event occurred|at 1721 this evening. Fire crews were dispatched to provide|suppression if needed. Wheatland Electric was able to repair and|restore power quickly. Power was restored around 1830. The Emergency Manager also stated  I really believe that it was the rapid fall in temperature that caused the connector to break and the power line to fall. 20 MPH shouldn't have caused that.  Kansas Mesonet site (TUNK1) near Tribune reported falling wind chills with the front around a 21 degree drop in 3 hours. Wind gusts at that site were gusting 20-23 mph during the time fram.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow band developed across northwest Kansas during the morning hours of February 21st leading to 3 to 7 inches of snow, along with 5.5 inches of snowfall at the NWS in Goodland, and 7 inches of snow occurring 8 N Brewster. Extremely hazardous driving conditions occurred along the roadways, including Interstate 70 in Sherman county where numerous slide offs occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Northwest Kansas KDOT Facebook page posted a webcam image at Mile Marker 22 in Sherman county showing snow packed roads. The post also stated that very slick conditions were ongoing along with less than 0.25 mile visibilities due to the heavy snow. Multiple slide offs were also reported as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across the area leading to multiple hail reports, with the largest being 2 inches in diameter located in Wheeler and 5 S of St. Francis. Severe wind gusts were also reported as well, with the strongest being 63 mph 9 NNW of McDonald. Multiple tornadoes also occurred as well in Sherman, Cheyenne, and Gove counties.  The town of Grinnell was directly hit by a strong tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado formed south of farmstead near River Road, made a glancing blow to the farmstead, then continued NNW toward Hwy 36.  Power poles were broken, a well established cotton wood tree was uprooted, and a well anchored antenna was twisted and bent by the tornado. Another farmstead north of CR M was also impacted by the tornado. The tornado removed the roof and doors from a metal building, relocated a couple grain bins, and leveled a wooden shed. However the tornado turned away from house before continuing north through the windbreak.  The tornado ended before reaching the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across the area leading to multiple hail reports, with the largest being 2 inches in diameter located in Wheeler and 5 S of St. Francis. Severe wind gusts were also reported as well, with the strongest being 63 mph 9 NNW of McDonald. Multiple tornadoes also occurred as well in Sherman, Cheyenne, and Gove counties.  The town of Grinnell was directly hit by a strong tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant tornado developed south-southwest of Grinnell west of the CR S and 22 intersection, then moved north-northeast across I-70 and over the western four blocks of the town of Grinnell before dissipating in a wheatfield north of the Grinnell Cow Paddy Golf Club.  The tornado caused damage to farmstead at CR X and 26, and east of the CR Z and 26 intersection.  Damage included moving round bales, leveling wooden sheds, pushing farm equipment and vehicles, and breaking large tree limbs.  Roughly 40 houses in Grinnell were damaged or destroyed, along with quonsets or other sheds.  Notable damage included moving a house roughly four inches off the foundation, debris piercing the grain bins on the north side of town, causing thousands of bushels of grain to spill out, complete destruction of a house built in the 1800s, and moving a couple graveside head stones a couple hundred yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across the area leading to multiple hail reports, with the largest being 2 inches in diameter located in Wheeler and 5 S of St. Francis. Severe wind gusts were also reported as well, with the strongest being 63 mph 9 NNW of McDonald. Multiple tornadoes also occurred as well in Sherman, Cheyenne, and Gove counties.  The town of Grinnell was directly hit by a strong tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same supercell which produced Grinnell tornado produced another EF3 tornado in Sheridan County, which led to damage to portions of a homestead. The worst damage was broken bolts and metal brackets of a metal frame that was bolted into concrete.  The tornado also removed the roof from a barn nearby, as well as leveling a cinderblock garage and removing/destroying wooden and metal grain bins.  The tornado started just northwest of CR 110 S and 40W based on Sentinel satellite data, crossed CR 90 S and 80 S, then lifted just northwest of County Road 406 and CR 20 W intersection based on the same Sentinel data as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening a group of thunderstorms moved northeast through part of Northwest Kansas.  The main threat with these storms was straight-line winds with the storm activity, which continued for some locations after the thunderstorms had ended.  The straight-line winds from these thunderstorms continued to produce an expanding outflow boundary as the storm activity moved in Kansas.  Wind gusts of 60-86 MPH were reported as the outflow boundary moved through, with gusts around 70 MPH reported even after the thunderstorm activity had moved on.  The highest wind gust of 86 MPH was reported north of Ludell.  With the dry soils, dust storm conditions were reported as the outflow boundary moved through.  The dust storm lasted for roughly 30 minutes before ending.  A few of the thunderstorms managed to produce large hail, with the largest being half dollar size at St. Francis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of an eighth of a mile visibility in a wall of dust along thunderstorm outflow boundary moving east through the county.  Meanwhile thunderstorm activity continued to move northeast over the northwest part of the Rawlins County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a mid-level front and an upper-level shortwave provided the lift needed to generate a line of storms. Given plenty of moisture, a line of storms were able to develop and strengthen. Once reaching the center of the CWA, a large enough cold pool developed to create a bowing segment with higher winds resulting in severe wind reports and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 8 to 10 inch tree branches snapped via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak outflow boundary from morning storms helped trigger storm development. Enough CAPE and shear (3000 j/kg and 40 kts) were present to support strong supercellular development. These supercells were prolific hailers and two cells generated giant (3.5-4 inch) hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of 1.25 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A regional outbreak of tornadoes occurred across portions|of western into south-central Kansas during the afternoon to|evening hours of Sunday 18 May 2025.  The first couple of tornadic|supercells occurred near Scott City KS in Scott County during the|late afternoon and progressed northward with both storms generating|EF2 tornadoes and a lone brief EFU tornado. A second tornadic|supercell developed near Dodge City KS in the early evening and|progressed northeast with an EFO tornado occurring near Kinsley KS|in Edwards County and an EF2 tornado near Garfield KS in Pawnee County.|A final long-duration tornadic supercell then formed in the mid|evening hours over Comanche County KS and commenced moving northeast|toward Greensburg KS in Kiowa County, Iuka KS in Pratt County,|north of Preston KS in Pratt County and finally across Stafford|and Reno Counties. This storm generated five EF3 tornadoes along with|several smaller satellite tornadoes.  The final count for this day|was 13 tornadoes just within the NWS Dodge City Warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: First tornado of this prolific cyclic supercell with the vortex starting in far northern Comanche County KS and then traveling north-northeast for over 8 miles before ending around 7 miles southeast of Mullinville KS in Kiowa County KS. Maximum width of almost a half mile with peak winds of at least 155 mph (EF3).  ||A farm grain cart was tossed/tumbled at least 100 yards, Total destruction of a metal building system. Trees debarked with only stubs of largest branches remaining. Electrical transmission lines were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense late afternoon and early thunderstorms produced large hail and localized flash flooding across a part of north central Kansas. Beloit was hit the hardest as hail reached 2��� in diameter. However, flash flooding thanks to nearly 6��� of rain was the problem in parts of Jewell and Smith counties. Smith County Emergency Management verified county roads flooded north of Lebanon along with water impacting U.S. Highway 281 north of Smith Center. The thunderstorms fired between 4 and 6 pm along in Osborne and Mitchell counties and rolled northeast. Despite the movement of the thunderstorms, eastern Smith and western/northern Jewell county experienced a 2-3 hour period of training thunderstorms dumping heavy rain which resulted in the flooding. Most of the runoff from the rain was in the White Rock Creek Basin and eventually flowed into Lovewell Reservoir. The thunderstorms lifted north into Nebraska by mid-evening. ||Meteorologically speaking, an upper level low pressure system was located in northeast Wyoming. A shortwave moved across Kansas and into south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours. Surface low pressure in northwest Kansas had weakened some, but there was still ample south and southeast flow to transport low level moisture north resulting in surface dewpoints in the middle 60s as far north as north central Kansas.  Though the deeper low-level moisture and surface instability was located in southern Kansas, strong frontogeneses in the 850-700 mb layer ahead of the short wave sparked a round of storms in the afternoon.  Steep mid-level lapses (8C/KM) and strong effective bulk shear of 60 knots was quite favorable for rapid thunderstorm development. Closer to the instability axis near the Kansas/Nebraska border, large hail from elevated thunderstorms was the primary hazard initially, but slightly drier air to the north (sub 60-degree surface dewpoints) supported a localized wind damage risk with a second round of thunderstorms even after dark. Though the risk for tornadoes was greater to the south, strong low-level wind shear and an eventual transition to bowing line segments did become more favorable for brief tornadoes to form for a time.

Though the risk for tornadoes was greater to the south, strong low-level wind shear and an eventual transition to bowing line segments did become more favorable for brief tornadoes to form for a time.

Intense late afternoon and early thunderstorms produced large hail and localized flash flooding across a part of north central Kansas. Beloit was hit the hardest as hail reached 2��� in diameter. Despite the movement of the thunderstorms, eastern Smith and western/northern Jewell county experienced a 2-3 hour period of training thunderstorms dumping heavy rain which resulted in the flooding. Most of the runoff from the rain was in the White Rock Creek Basin and eventually flowed into Lovewell Reservoir. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado began just south of State Highway 96, southwest of Modoc. The tornado traveled northeast and immediately snapped power poles along the highway. As it continued northeastward, some train cars were toppled just west of town. To the north end of Modoc, a mobile home had its roof ripped off. As the tornado continued tracking northeastward in open fields, it continued producing sporadic damage to power lines, a home, and pivot irrigation systems. The tornado dissipated just north of W Road 200.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a six-month respite (since mid-November 2024) with no worse than Moderate Drought (D1) assigned to any portion of this six-county North Central Kansas area by the U.S. Drought Monitor (USDM), late-May 2025 technically saw the return of Severe Drought (D2) into far northwestern Phillips County, where recent and year-to-date precipitation deficits were most pronounced within the area. Fortunately, the May drought situation was not as severe elsewhere in North Central Kansas, with the bulk of the six counties assigned no worse than Abnormally Dry (D0). Taking a closer look at USDM drought category details within North Central Kansas during May, and as just mentioned, the vast majority of the area either maintained or even improved to D0 during the month...with approximately 77 percent of real estate (including all of Rooks/Phillips counties) assigned D0 at month's end. Meanwhile, roughly 21% of the domain (highest coverage in Jewell County) sported Moderate Drought (D1) at the end of May. The remainder of the six-county area...less than 2% of it...contained the aforementioned D2 that was introduced into far northwestern Phillips County on the May 27th USDM issuance. ||Turning to May 2025 precipitation details, and leaning heavily on radar estimation along with observations from 27 NWS and CoCoRaHS observers, it was an overall-dry...but not truly notably-dry...month within North Central Kansas as a whole. Roughly 93% of the area saw at least slightly below normal precipitation (most places totaled between 2.00-3.80), but only around 11% of the area picked up less than half-normal (normal May precipitation being around 4.17 inches). Although ground-truth gauge measurements were few, Phillips County was clearly the overall-driest of the six counties, with official totals including: 1.36 in Phillipsburg, 1.90 six miles east of Phillipsburg and 2.43 at Kirwin Dam. In Phillipsburg, this marked the driest May in 11 years (since 0.67 in 2014). Broadening out the scope beyond May itself, year-to-date precipitation within North Central Kansas was a mixed bag. As of the end of May, most of the area stood somewhere between only slightly below normal and even slightly above normal for the year (overall-highest coverage of slightly above normal within parts of southern Osborne/Rooks/Mitchell counties). However, more pronounced year-to-date dryness was noted within especially Phillips/Smith counties. At Phillipsburg, the January-May total of only 4.88 was 3.77 below normal (56% of normal) and marked the lowest year-to-date total in 11 years (since 4.81 in 2014).

Widely scattered storms developed in within an area of robust instability and weak shear leading to short-lived severe weather in very localized areas. With that being said, storms managed to produce up to golf ball sized hail along with up to 70 mph wind gusts across portions of south-central Kansas and the Flint Hills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A KAKE storm spotter reported a few quarter sized hail stones in south part of Arkansas  City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported 2.75 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of storms developed during the evening hours and continued into the overnight in north central and south central Kansas. Strong and damaging winds were the main hazard with estimated wind gusts up to 75 mph. There was damage to trees and powerlines reported. A semi trailer was blown over on Interstate 70 as well. A few of the storms had hail from nickel to quarter size. These storms were efficient rainfall producers with flooding in the western portions of Saline County which closed many roadways as 7.45 inches fell in Brookville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust from a Union Pacific weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was the impetus for surface based thunderstorm development that afternoon. An initial, single supercell developed over Rush county around 5 pm, before uncommonly  moving southwest through Dodge city and Meade through 10 PM. Additional organized thunderstorms were lifted by the cold front, in an northeast to southwest line and swept across southwest and central Kansas between about 10 pm and 2 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms moved into northwest Kansas from eastern Colorado during the evening of the 6th.  These storms produced numerous reports of severe wind, with the strongest being a recorded 82 mph gust in eastern Sherman county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of storms developed during the evening hours and continued into the overnight in north central and south central Kansas. Strong and damaging winds were the main hazard with estimated wind gusts up to 75 mph. There was damage to trees and powerlines reported. A semi trailer was blown over on Interstate 70 as well. A few of the storms had hail from nickel to quarter size. These storms were efficient rainfall producers with flooding in the western portions of Saline County which closed many roadways as 7.45 inches fell in Brookville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts around 60 mph along with nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms moved northward out of Oklahoma and into south-central Kansas during the early afternoon hours of July 18th. This cluster, while isolated, produced up to 71 mph winds in the Wichita metro along with wind damage across the city. Further south, up to golf ball sized hail was reported in Conway Springs. As storms continued northward during the early evening hours, they began to lose their intensity. However, 60 mph winds were reported in Marion County before storms became sub-severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initially reported as estimated 60 mph wind gusts, the winds continued increasing until it was estimated that wind gusts were around 70 mph a few minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms and outflow winds from thunderstorms led to numerous severe wind gusts across northwest Kansas and blowing dust with visibilities around one quarter mile. The strongest wind gust was 76 mph located 9 WSW Winona.  Penny size hail was reported in McDonald.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of blowing dust associated with thunderstorm outflow wind reducing visibility to less than one half mile. Wind gusts of 53 mph was recorded at the Goodland Airport with the outflow, and 60 mph was reported at the Burlington Airport as well with the outflow. Further downstream reports around 70mph were also recorded with winds from the thunderstorm outflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms moved northward out of Oklahoma and into south-central Kansas during the early afternoon hours of July 18th. This cluster, while isolated, produced up to 71 mph winds in the Wichita metro along with wind damage across the city. Further south, up to golf ball sized hail was reported in Conway Springs. As storms continued northward during the early evening hours, they began to lose their intensity. However, 60 mph winds were reported in Marion County before storms became sub-severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported tree damage on Zoo Blvd just east of I-235, part of the lane was being blocked by the downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of storms developed during the evening hours and continued into the overnight in north central and south central Kansas. Strong and damaging winds were the main hazard with estimated wind gusts up to 75 mph. There was damage to trees and powerlines reported. A semi trailer was blown over on Interstate 70 as well. A few of the storms had hail from nickel to quarter size. These storms were efficient rainfall producers with flooding in the western portions of Saline County which closed many roadways as 7.45 inches fell in Brookville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated winds of 65 to 70 mph and a large tree limb was knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong northwesterly winds overspread portions of central and south-central Kansas during the morning hours of December 18th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds will measured at the Salina Airport from 12:30 PM to 3:00 PM. Sustained speeds were between 50-60 mph with gusts up to 71 mph. Wind knocked down a large street lamp post along S 9th Street in Salina which was relayed by KSAL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough was situated such that strong ascent will be supported. While the strongest surface forcing is farther west, enough convection initiated and moving eastward into the CWA. While staying discrete at first, it quickly developed into a MCS. Through the middle of the night a cohesive cold pool developed and generated 2 80+ mph wind gust reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station recorded a wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the evening of the 27th, scattered storms formed across northwestern Kansas. These storms were generally slow moving, high precipitation storms with a couple stronger embedded storms that produced one inch hail and 60 MPH winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Still hailing at the time of the report. Hail was to the size of quarters along with winds gusting to 45 to 50 mph and heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powdery snow fell on Monday December 1st across northeast Kansas.  It was the first snow of the year and fell on a Monday morning after a holiday weekend so travel impacts were greater than perhaps would have been expected.   Most areas received 1-4 inches however a band of 4-5 inch amounts did occur around the Topeka to Lawrence corridor into Jefferson County.   Winds however were light under 10 mph so this could have been worse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 5.2 inches of snow measured in McClouth in eastern Jefferson County.

The wind occurred on the northern side of a storm where a heat burst occurred as temperatures rose from the low 80s into the low 100s over a 20 minute span, and dew points fell from the low 60s to the mid 40s according to the KKSSELDE3 site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak outflow boundary from morning storms helped trigger storm development. Enough CAPE and shear (3000 j/kg and 40 kts) were present to support strong supercellular development. These supercells were prolific hailers and two cells generated giant (3.5-4 inch) hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of about 3.5 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave moved across the Great plains and supported the development of supercells. There was only a small window for severe weather from each supercell moved south before weakening. After all the cells had weakened below severe and developed a small cold pool, they congealed and followed a more linear structure that tracked east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kansas Mesonet site - Hays recorded a 62 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave developed and was able to initiate ascent and allow storms access CAPE of over 2000 J/kg and modest bulk shear. This generated a handful of marginal supercells. Two of them became strong enough to generate severe reports of wind and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media report of 1.75 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary initiated some storms that had access with shear up to 40 KTs and CAPE above 3000 J/kg. While beginning as a cluster of storms along the boundary, it quickly developed a more linear structure. The southern edge had access to a more favorable environment and was able to become severe from around in the late night before weaking considerably after midnight. Wind gusts with the storms were as high as 94 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported up to 6 inch tree limbs broken. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall moved across portions of south-central Kansas during the morning hours of August 28th. This heavy rain resulted in the flooding of the Kansas Turnpike near Wellington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dispatcher reported water over Interstate 35 somewhere between mile markers 19 and 21. The interstate was closed for a period of time to allow the water to recede. One accident was reported from water over the roadway along Highway 81 west of Belle Plaine with unfortunately six injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate clusters of thunderstorms moved across portions of central and south-central Kansas during the afternoon hours of August 19. These storms produced multiple reports of large hail up to half dollar size and wind gusts up to 72 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report was received via mPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moved over western Kansas early in the morning. Correlating with a PVU maximum and left exit region of the jet aloft, plenty of dynamical support was present for strong storms to develop producing all hazards. This included a weak and brief tornado, 4 inch hail, and 85 mph winds. This manifested in a couple supercells and a small bowing MCS moving southward across SW Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two public reports came in at the same time reporting multiple large tree limbs downed. One of the report included pictures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary initiated some storms that had access with shear up to 40 KTs and CAPE above 3000 J/kg. While beginning as a cluster of storms along the boundary, it quickly developed a more linear structure. The southern edge had access to a more favorable environment and was able to become severe from around in the late night before weaking considerably after midnight. Wind gusts with the storms were as high as 94 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KJHN reported wind gust 94 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powdery snow fell on Monday December 1st across northeast Kansas.  It was the first snow of the year and fell on a Monday morning after a holiday weekend so travel impacts were greater than perhaps would have been expected.   Most areas received 1-4 inches however a band of 4-5 inch amounts did occur around the Topeka to Lawrence corridor into Jefferson County.   Winds however were light under 10 mph so this could have been worse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four and seven tenths inches (4.7) of snow was measured at NWS Topeka.   Other measurements around the city of Topeka included 5 to 5.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large trough was situated across much of the western CONUS. This set up a potent baroclinic zone draped diagonally from the SW to the NE. Mid-level lapse rates were around 7-8 C. Bulk shear was over 40 KT with sufficient CAPE. In the late afternoon storms began to fire along the stalled boundary. These supercells primarily produced 70-81 mph winds with an additional 1 inch hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of a 65 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high wind gusts up to 85 mph across northwest Kansas during the evening of the 17th into the early morning of the 18th. Numerous wind gusts of 58-80+ mph were observed, with the strongest being just west of Goodland topping out at 85 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ambient weather site named Section 17 located 10 NE Wallace recorded multiple instances of 81 mph wind gusts and a gust of 78 mph before the 81 mph gusts. The Kansas Mesonet site named Russell Springs 3 SW, and a weather underground site named KKSWALLA4 located 13 SSE Wallace both recorded a 64 mph wind gust. A 59 and 61 mph wind gust was also recorded at the Oakley AWOS (KOEL).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts ranging from 58-81 mph occurred across northwest Kansas. The strongest wind gust of 81 mph was located 10 NE Wallace in Logan county. Zero visibility in blowing dust was also reported 3 NE Seguin in Sheridan county which led to the closure of Highway 24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ambient Weather Station named Hendricks_1420_Road25 which is located 4 WNW Bird City recorded the 65 mph wind gust; the same site also recorded a 63, 60 and 58 mph gust. The WeatherUnderground site named KKSSAINT20 located in St. Francis recorded wind gusts of 62 mph on three occasions. The Kansas Mesonet site located 10 S St Francis recorded wind gusts of 59 and 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WeatherUnderground station named KKSOBERL22 located 3 ENE Herndon recorded a 69 mph wind gust. The same site also had wind gusts of 60 and 62 mph. The Kansas Mesonet site named Oberlin 7 NE recorded a 65 mph wind gust. The Oberlin Airport AWOS (KOIN) recorded a 62 mph wind gust. Ambient Weather site named Dan_Wasson recorded a 59 mph wind gust which is located in Oberlin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kansas Mesonet site named Gove5SE recorded a 60 mph wind gust. A KDOT weather site named K5OK7 I70 recorded wind gusts of 59 mph on two occasions. An Ambient Weather site located 10 S Gove also recorded a 57 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A portion of Interstate 70 closed the morning of the 4th due to very slick conditions, and a report of multiple slide offs due to freezing fog and freezing drizzle overnight.  That evening melting snow refroze compounding the already slick roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sherman county dispatch reported multiple slide offs due to very slick conditions along Interstate 70. The slick conditions were from re-freeze of light snow from earlier in the day, followed by brief periods of freezing drizzle, freezing rain and freezing fog. The NWS in Goodland measured 0.02 inches of ice accumulation from all of this combined at the office. Interstate 70 was closed from Goodland to the Kansas/Colorado line for around an hour the morning of the 5th due to the conditions.||Key Impacts:  slick roadways, icy driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high wind gusts up to 85 mph across northwest Kansas during the evening of the 17th into the early morning of the 18th. Numerous wind gusts of 58-80+ mph were observed, with the strongest being just west of Goodland topping out at 85 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 62-79 mph were recorded at the St. Francis Airport (KSYF) AWOS. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing Fog and some freezing drizzle developed across northwest Kansas. Light ice amounts were reported across northwest Kansas with the exception of a social media report with a picture of a ruler with around 0.25-0.3 inches of flat ice accretion on the side of a rain gauge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter with a picture on Facebook measured flat ice accumulation on elevated surfaces generally around an eighth of an inch. Other objects in the picture suggest accumulations around 0.25 to 0.3 inches are possible.   Impacts: Light ice accretion, slick surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high wind gusts up to 85 mph across northwest Kansas during the evening of the 17th into the early morning of the 18th. Numerous wind gusts of 58-80+ mph were observed, with the strongest being just west of Goodland topping out at 85 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AM radio tower 3 WSW Goodland with this initial round of winds was leaning from the high winds then was damaged enough to no longer broadcast with the second round of winds during the day on the 18th.  The leaning tower finally collapsed on the 27th on a breezy day.  A personal weather station of a former NWS employee recorded a wind gust of 85 mph 2 ENE Caruso. The Goodland Airport ASOS recorded wind gusts of 69-84 mph. Sustained winds were 58 mph at the time of 84 mph wind gust. A weather underground site named KKSKANOR6 located 13 NNW Ruleton recorded a 76 mph wind gust as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning dense fog was in place over Norton County.  A crash involving two vehicles occurred due to the low visibility in the fog.  One person sustained minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two vehicle crashed occurred on Highway 383 north of Norton during the morning in dense fog.  A semi was stopped on the highway, waiting to turn into a road.  A driver coming up from behind struck the semi.  One driver sustained minor injuries.  The crash closed the highway between Highway 36 and the Nebraska border for two hours due to clean up occurring in the dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving through the area during the late evening hours of the 28th led to light snow and icy roads as temperatures steadily dropped. A single vehicle accident occurred in northern Norton county due to the icy roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, and north winds gusting up to 40 mph in wake of a strong cold front led to icy roads across the county. Snow amounts were generally light with COCORAHS amounts showing around 0.5 inches with the snow ending across Norton county around 6am CT. A single vehicle crash occurred on K-383, about 2.7 miles north of US 36. The vehicle with 5 occupants hit a patch of ice, which lost control and flipped into a a north ditch. An 11 year old occupant sustained minor injuries.||Key Impacts:  icy roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Tuesday June 17th, a line of storms developed into a bow echo as it progressed south. This line of storms produced extremely strong winds as it tracked south, with Hutchinson reporting an 89 mph gust and the Wichita Airport seeing a 102 mph gust! This was the strongest wind gust ever reported at the Wichita Airport.||These extreme winds caused extensive damage with Sedgwick County receiving around 250 reports of power lines being down which resulted in around 85,000 residents with out power. The stronger wind gusts around 50 to 60 mph lasted for around an hour. ||Additional storms developed throughout the day producing wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph,  near four inch hail in Arkansas City and a brief tornado in Harper County. Tree damage was extensive throughout much of Wichita and surrounding areas especially with multiple rounds of strong winds. The hail in Arkansas City was baseball to softball sized which led to damage of vehicles, homes and businesses across town.

An intense supercell thunderstorm developed over portions of Saline County during the late afternoon hours of September 3rd. This storm weakened as a second supercell thunderstorm developed across portions of McPherson County. This storm moved across portions of McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick and produced several reports of large hail up to baseball size. A third supercell storm developed across southwest McPherson County and once again, tracked across Harvey and Sedgwick counties. Large hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 70 mph were reported. Extensive damage to roofs, trees, siding, and vehicles occurred across McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a roof panel on a gas station was blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity spread east into Northwest Kansas from Colorado.  Hail up to tennis ball size was reported near Sharon Springs. During the early morning hours of Tuesday June 17th, a line of storms developed into a bow echo as it progressed south. This line of storms produced extremely strong winds as it tracked south, with Hutchinson reporting an 89 mph gust and the Wichita Airport seeing a 102 mph gust! This was the strongest wind gust ever reported at the Wichita Airport.||These extreme winds caused extensive damage with Sedgwick County receiving around 250 reports of power lines being down which resulted in around 85,000 residents with out power. The stronger wind gusts around 50 to 60 mph lasted for around an hour. ||Additional storms developed throughout the day producing wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph,  near four inch hail in Arkansas City and a brief tornado in Harper County. Tree damage was extensive throughout much of Wichita and surrounding areas especially with multiple rounds of strong winds. The hail in Arkansas City was baseball to softball sized which led to damage of vehicles, homes and businesses across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at the AWOS station at Lyons Rice County Municipal Airport. Winds gusted up to 60 mph for at least 20 minutes between 2:55 AM CDT and 3:15 AM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense supercell thunderstorm developed over portions of Saline County during the late afternoon hours of September 3rd. This storm weakened as a second supercell thunderstorm developed across portions of McPherson County. This storm moved across portions of McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick and produced several reports of large hail up to baseball size. A third supercell storm developed across southwest McPherson County and once again, tracked across Harvey and Sedgwick counties. Large hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 70 mph were reported. Extensive damage to roofs, trees, siding, and vehicles occurred across McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick counties.

In the early afternoon of the 19th, a cold front moving over northwestern Kansas caused  a few isolated storms to form that generally moved south-southeast, except one supercell that moved almost due east over Oberlin then moved south.  This single storm was the source for the storm reports. Hail up to 2.25 inches were reported, the largest falling in eastern Sheridan County.  The strongest reported gust was 69 MPH near Quinter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kksquint19 weather underground.

An intense supercell thunderstorm developed over portions of Saline County during the late afternoon hours of September 3rd. This storm weakened as a second supercell thunderstorm developed across portions of McPherson County. This storm moved across portions of McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick and produced several reports of large hail up to baseball size. A third supercell storm developed across southwest McPherson County and once again, tracked across Harvey and Sedgwick counties. Large hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 70 mph were reported. Extensive damage to roofs, trees, siding, and vehicles occurred across McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick counties.

A small area of heavy rain developed across central Kansas during the morning of September 8 and moved little into the early afternoon. Portions of southwest Ottawa County saw five to nine inches of rain, resulting in notable flooding in and around Tescott. County officials estimated almost 5 million dollars in damage total - 3.5 million dollars in the city of Tescott itself, 1.2 million dollars in the rest of the county, and 90000 dollars in damage for the public school district buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few streets flooded in Culver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 16th, two lines of storms formed, one in northwestern Kansas, the other in eastern Colorado. The northwestern Kansas storms slowly moved to the north-northeast as the Colorado line moved to the northeast. As these two lines collided, additional storms formed near the I-70 corridor. Storms lasted for over 5 hours, produced winds up to 80 to 90 MPH, causing damage at a brick car wash in St. Francis and causing visibility to drop to near zero from blowing dust. Additionally, hail up to 1 inch was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated 70 to 80 mph winds with blowing dust creating visibility reductions  of 1/8 to 1/2 half mile. Also reported a 12 inch and multiple 8 inch tree limb broken. Some 8 inch limbs were thrown 300 to 350 feet from trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Tuesday June 17th, a line of storms developed into a bow echo as it progressed south. This line of storms produced extremely strong winds as it tracked south, with Hutchinson reporting an 89 mph gust and the Wichita Airport seeing a 102 mph gust! This was the strongest wind gust ever reported at the Wichita Airport.||These extreme winds caused extensive damage with Sedgwick County receiving around 250 reports of power lines being down which resulted in around 85,000 residents with out power. The stronger wind gusts around 50 to 60 mph lasted for around an hour. ||Additional storms developed throughout the day producing wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph,  near four inch hail in Arkansas City and a brief tornado in Harper County. Tree damage was extensive throughout much of Wichita and surrounding areas especially with multiple rounds of strong winds. The hail in Arkansas City was baseball to softball sized which led to damage of vehicles, homes and businesses across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be around 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense supercell thunderstorm developed over portions of Saline County during the late afternoon hours of September 3rd. This storm weakened as a second supercell thunderstorm developed across portions of McPherson County. This storm moved across portions of McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick and produced several reports of large hail up to baseball size. A third supercell storm developed across southwest McPherson County and once again, tracked across Harvey and Sedgwick counties. Large hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 70 mph were reported. Extensive damage to roofs, trees, siding, and vehicles occurred across McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick counties.

Thunderstorms expanded in coverage during the afternoon hours on the 14th. These storms produced brief gusty outflow winds and potential for a few brief landspout tornadoes. A brief tornado northeast of El Dorado which did move over the lake was captured from the Kansas Turnpike. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture was taken of a brief tornado that moved over El Dorado Lake. This picture was taken from the Kansas Turnpike and shared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 16th, two lines of storms formed, one in northwestern Kansas, the other in eastern Colorado. The northwestern Kansas storms slowly moved to the north-northeast as the Colorado line moved to the northeast. As these two lines collided, additional storms formed near the I-70 corridor. Storms lasted for over 5 hours, produced winds up to 80 to 90 MPH, causing damage at a brick car wash in St. Francis and causing visibility to drop to near zero from blowing dust. Additionally, hail up to 1 inch was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station at the Oberlin airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms slowly moved across southeast Kansas during the early morning hours of June 28th. These storms produced very heavy rainfall and reports of flash flooding in the vicinity of Mound Valley, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported to law enforcement that water was covering Highway 160 near Mound  Valley. In addition, law enforcement reported a high-water rescue in the vicinity of 13000 and Elk roads.

Numerous reports of flash-flooding were observed across the area with significant flooding across Sedgwick and Butler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire department reported street flooding in Potwin and Butler Road approximately 3 miles south of Whitewater which was impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 16th, two lines of storms formed, one in northwestern Kansas, the other in eastern Colorado. The northwestern Kansas storms slowly moved to the north-northeast as the Colorado line moved to the northeast. As these two lines collided, additional storms formed near the I-70 corridor. Storms lasted for over 5 hours, produced winds up to 80 to 90 MPH, causing damage at a brick car wash in St. Francis and causing visibility to drop to near zero from blowing dust. Additionally, hail up to 1 inch was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Tuesday June 17th, a line of storms developed into a bow echo as it progressed south. This line of storms produced extremely strong winds as it tracked south, with Hutchinson reporting an 89 mph gust and the Wichita Airport seeing a 102 mph gust! This was the strongest wind gust ever reported at the Wichita Airport.||These extreme winds caused extensive damage with Sedgwick County receiving around 250 reports of power lines being down which resulted in around 85,000 residents with out power. The stronger wind gusts around 50 to 60 mph lasted for around an hour. ||Additional storms developed throughout the day producing wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph,  near four inch hail in Arkansas City and a brief tornado in Harper County. Tree damage was extensive throughout much of Wichita and surrounding areas especially with multiple rounds of strong winds. The hail in Arkansas City was baseball to softball sized which led to damage of vehicles, homes and businesses across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust from Beech Factory Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across south-central Kansas during the morning hours of June 3rd and slowly moved to the northeast. Additional rounds of thunderstorms developed and trained across portions of south-central and southeast Kansas. Numerous reports of flash-flooding were observed across the area with significant flooding across Sedgwick and Butler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of significant flash flooding were reported across portions of Butler County. These reports included, but not limited to, the following road closures: Kansas Highway 77 at McCollum, Highway 254 in El Dorado and Prairie Creek Road near Benton. Many other side streets and roads across Butler County were also impassible especially in El Dorado. In addition to the road closures, numerous reports of stalled vehicles due to high water were reported across many areas of the county. Homes were  evacuated on the north and southwest side of El Dorado due to rapidly rising flood waters. Flooding on area roadways and into homes impacted the Wagon Wheel neighborhood near Santa Fe Lake. A total of 240 homes and businesses were impacted in the county with damage to county roadways as well resulting in $4 million infrastructure damages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of significant flash flooding were reported across portions of Sedgwick County. These reports included but not limited to the following road closures: several stretches of Kellogg in the Wichita metro area and the intersection of 13th Street and Webb. Many other side streets and roads across metro Wichita and the county were impassible. In addition to the road closures, numerous reports of stalled vehicles due to high water were reported across many areas of metro Wichita and county. Multiple homes and apartments had water enter them due to rapidly rising flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of Tuesday June 17th, a line of storms developed into a bow echo as it progressed south. This line of storms produced extremely strong winds as it tracked south, with Hutchinson reporting an 89 mph gust and the Wichita Airport seeing a 102 mph gust! This was the strongest wind gust ever reported at the Wichita Airport.||These extreme winds caused extensive damage with Sedgwick County receiving around 250 reports of power lines being down which resulted in around 85,000 residents with out power. The stronger wind gusts around 50 to 60 mph lasted for around an hour. ||Additional storms developed throughout the day producing wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph,  near four inch hail in Arkansas City and a brief tornado in Harper County. Tree damage was extensive throughout much of Wichita and surrounding areas especially with multiple rounds of strong winds. The hail in Arkansas City was baseball to softball sized which led to damage of vehicles, homes and businesses across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was snapped and leaning onto a house.

An intense supercell thunderstorm developed over portions of Saline County during the late afternoon hours of September 3rd. This storm weakened as a second supercell thunderstorm developed across portions of McPherson County. This storm moved across portions of McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick and produced several reports of large hail up to baseball size. A third supercell storm developed across southwest McPherson County and once again, tracked across Harvey and Sedgwick counties. Large hail up to baseball size and wind gusts in excess of 70 mph were reported. Extensive damage to roofs, trees, siding, and vehicles occurred across McPherson, Harvey, and Sedgwick counties.

A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed across south central Kansas during the late afternoon hours of September 1st. These storms then moving south producing several reports of large hail across Kingman and Harper counties with damaging winds measured in southwest Sedgwick County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches up to 10 inches in diameter were reported down. A car port was flipped, and a power line was down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over Northwest Kansas in the late afternoon and early evening.  A thunderstorm moving over Atwood had the largest hail reported; golf ball size.  Elsewhere wind gusts around 60 MPH were measured at St. Francis and Oberlin.  A dust storm associated with thunderstorm winds was reported north of Blakeman along a broken line of thunderstorms as the storms moved north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of 1 3/4 inch hail currently falling  in Atwood.  The hail ranged from quarter to golf ball in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved from northern Oklahoma into southern Kansas during the early morning hours of September 23rd. A report of damaging winds was relayed from Harper County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon of the 22nd, storms began developing off a east/west oriented boundary, south of I-70. The boundary soon filled with storms that produced hail up to 1.25 inches, 61 MPH winds, and flooding by the morning of the 23rd.  The largest hail reported fell in Sharon Springs, the strongest wind gust was in southern Sherman County, and the flooding happened Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather Link Site Delta Dos report of 61 MPH gust.
